reviews.episodeName: In the Wake of Repeated Spirals

reviews.reviewText: I love this show so much. It is so wholesome and dramatic at the same time. Rooting for William, period.

There’s something incredibly heartwarming and wholesome about this show. While it’s ostensibly a gay male dating reality show, it really seems much more about the various journeys gay men take to and through those relationships. At no point did I think any of these men were a match for another, but that’s also very life. The framing of the show is refreshing to American eyes, and simultaneously disconcerting. The reunion episode, in particular, seemed to betray the zone of comfort I prefer in reality television. The hosts seemed to just be fans of the show and there was no actual processing of the unresolved information from the main episodes. In fact, the interview style left a sour taste in my mouth. It felt voyeuristic in a way that I could ignore during the main episodes, but could not at the point of a more “real” episode. Ultimately, is representation for the majority gaze worth it? I don’t really think so. But I would probably watch another season, so what do I know? Justice for Taeheon!

I think the general populous’s obsession with “true crime” has led to all these slop shows with not much to say. The twist at the end is really just the beginning of a conversation that ought to have been happening across the entire season.
Why do people not know how to delete texts fully?
A slightly different setup than I thought this would be. I can see where this is going from a mile away but anything for Tessa and Jon.

tmdb-tv-110381.name: The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City

tmdb.name: The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City

reviews.reviewText: The plane drama really tore this season apart and I don’t see how the case in this iteration moves forward. The reunion pushed so far through the fourth wall that I wasn’t sure why Andy was even there. I’m praying for a cast shakeup to restore this show to its true glory.
All the mothers on the right couch are awake!
Saggiterrorists are fully activated. Lightning in a bottle this show.

Mary Cosby was firing on all cylinders. Bronwyn carried. I don’t know how but Meredith gave Kim and Rinna vibes at the same time. I’m obsessed with how polarizing she and Lisa are as the Sagittarians of the group. That shady playwright read them to filth. Stellar episode, but the edit feels a bit contrived at this point.
reviews.episodeName: Sisters of Salt, a Greek Tragedy

reviews.reviewText: The stage whispering in the spa really got me. Also, Lisa’s edit was so rude because she is right about the trees. Have we not watched Avatar?

It’s super hard to outperform a knockout season. It’s even harder to seem like you’re not faltering when you don’t. This season of SLC managed to pull it off, though. Once again, well-edited, balanced in its treatment, and highly digestible. We love our camp emotionally grounded. Forever grateful that Mary Cosby and Angie K came fully locked into this season. I feel incredibly vindicated in my initial read on Bronwyn; I maintain that her husband is the only one clocking her tea. Sad to say, but this may be the last time we see daddy Seth on screen because mother Marks seems big done.
Well, Monica ate that. I am also Reality VonTease.

I think this family needs more help, but damn if I haven’t lived this. I loved the choice of filming the adult actors playing kids. And the further choice to have them do it earnestly and organically. Older, parentified children will rise! Emmys all around, to be perfectly honest.
It’s no shade, but a Frank Ocean needle drop won’t con me. A near perfect episode buoyed by Lili Reinhart.
This is such an unwell family. I could fix Hal, though.
I would’ve been here sooner if I had known about Havana Rose Liu’s involvement. Already falling in love with Hal.

There’s a brief period between episodes 2 and 3, where the show’s potential is highly visible. Unfortunately, they saw that and ran in the opposite direction. None of the urgency felt earned and the script felt convoluted.
Good soup…
Momentum fell off precipitously.
“The best tail is from the front,”I say as I follow this show into the dark.
Die Another Day mentioned.
Already lots of messy plot slop, but Simu is my BD so I will be continuing.

There’s so much money put into this show that it punches above its weight. At the end of the day, I watch for the actors. The meta performances through a contrived script had me more locked in than expected. I want Joe Keery and Charlie Heaton to kiss.
Makes you recall that the last few seasons could’ve been condensed.

Clarifies the Juancaraz breakup, but not necessarily a revolutionary documentary. I will say I admire how Carlos, as a person from a younger generation, resists burnout. Him crying broke me — he deserves to always be happy! I’m looking forward to the many other documentaries that will surely be created around his aura.
They found a better structure, and also found a way to make an evening more interesting tennis season just as boring as they did the prior one. Kinda glad we stopped here.

We bit off more than we could chew on this one. Too many players covered in too much of a scattershot approach. The natural narrative build of that season felt suppressed and spoiled the chance to welcome fans into the tennis community and such an inflection point where a new era creates a more inviting atmosphere than usual. You can’t help but feel that the players really just wanted a check — I guess I don’t blame them.

It’s been a minute since a show had me foaming at the mouth like this. I love gay shit! And autism representation. We have never been more back! The media moment around the show, in some ways, makes it what it is. Scott/François can never do any wrong in my book. And I hope Hudson never gets media training — I love my silly goofy spicy not gay boy! Until the foreseeable future, I’m mentally at the cottage.

I love a slow burn, but I think the season finale narrowly missed a meaningful payoff. Mostly, it feels like the stakes get flattened out. Even though Carol’s and Manousos’s worlds colliding is significant, none of the material facts have really changed for Carol as the protagonist. It’s really just that she decides to remember what she already knows. Aside from this, my current theory is that the show is an allegory for alcoholism and I enjoy watching through that lens. In some ways, this makes raising the stakes more difficult, especially given the literal plot; while I don’t think They would necessarily operate differently, I think seeing them encounter roadblocks, albeit silently, would create some needed tension for us as the audience. This all said, I could probably watch this show explore this world for quite a while.

It took me a while to get into the groove on this one, but the back half of the season had me locked in. At the end of the day, I love Rachel Sennott. I’m still mad that we couldn’t have a pure Josh Hutcherson for longer than we did. Alani always.

I continue to eat this show up. It is my water for December. And despite how pedestrian a saccharine it is, I continue to celebrate Philippine Leroy-Beaulieu finding way to deliver the most grounded performance amidst the chaos. I love how Ashley Park used this season to showcase more of the breadth of her talent — that shouldn’t go unremarked upon. Hello, Minnie Driver!
The season splitting really sapped the momentum. I don’t watch this show for it to be good, though. I watch it for the hot men, mostly slay fashion, and Sylvie. Cheers to Marcello entering the villa.
Another victim of a first season being made to justify a second one. But I was still unwell and cracking up.

Lots of hilariously over-the-top lines, but that’s not enough to hold together the plot. There’s so much oscillation between that tone and serious topics that it’s hard to know how to feel throughout. And beyond that, I can’t even say what the central plot really was?

One of Netflix’s stronger mini series entries in a while. Took me a few episodes to lock in, but then I was pretty seated. We still need to stop with the penultimate exposition episodes. It’s so contrived at this point that it must continue to be actively shamed. Also, can we get Hettienne Park meatier roles? Asking for me because I love her work and we need more of it.
It’s no Slow Horses, but I was definitely locked in. I wanted a bit more to wrestle with, but at least it was well-executed if not a bit straightforward.
I’m watching for hot, funny people. And that’s not even paying off at this point. Respectfully, it’s time to wrap it up.

Somehow this show doesn’t rise above meh. They set up such a great premise in season one that I had really hoped this follow-up would fly. Unfortunately, it maybe just hovers. While it’s shot like prestige comedy, it never rises above the level of a sitcom filler episode. At this, I think I’m just watching to look at Joel Kim Booster and his fits.

tmdb-tv-290885.name: Wife Swap: The Real Housewives Edition

tmdb.name: Wife Swap: The Real Housewives Edition

reviews.reviewText: Obviously the Angie episode was great, but then everything after flopped for me. Maybe because I don’t watch these franchises…
Not really a well-produced documentary and I find some of the filming of subjects to be ethically questionable. A horrifying and sad story, but it was in needed of better stewards maybe.

I cannot stand the pace of this show, even though I keep coming back. There are parts that seem very meandering and others that are so quick that I can’t enjoy them. A large part of this is the timeline jumping between the past and present; the cuts are just poorly timed and I think I maybe would’ve preferred a different overall structure that balances the timeline. Armand is a fucking narc. Lestat forever, I’m so sorry!

I’m glad I’m finally getting involved in this show, but it definitely takes a few episodes to get going. Finding myself through it, I’m left pondering just how long forever might feel. Gay yearning is at an all-time high in my zip code.

I love this slop. Possibly their most discordant season yet, but I am still here and locked in. Love seeing baddies take down baddies (here, Billy Crudup taking down Marion Cotillard). That said, let’s start wrapping it up.
Even if they’re guilty, I just think the defense is the only person who did their job. And the influence of the media on the minds of the people is, perhaps, the thing of note in this whole story.
The last two episodes were a doozy. The realism behind the absurdity in this season was palpable. Could’ve watched several more episodes of the action drama it became.
I’m curious to see where he could take his budding television career. This felt like the best parts of I Think You Should Leave and Random Acts of Flyness put together.

Took half a year to slog through this final season. I don’t know that the show took a swan dive as much as it became too real and thus hard to watch. I wouldn’t say it was ever escapist, but I think viewers wanted more of a “win” than the show ever delivered. And by the end, the white feminist tone of it all added to how grim our situation is in real life.

Incredibly glossy, but I wanted a touch more grit. For all the family drama, it just seems like a bunch of jackass men upsetting women who aren’t as frail as their dialogue sometimes suggests. The timeline was smartly deployed and the layers of mystery were nice to see come together, even if it was a bit obvious in some places. I need a mall shake like yesterday.

Chilling stuff, but as a documentary falls a little flat. It feels like every episode just scratches the surface, then backs off. And the end totally feels like there could be more episodes coming, which would be welcome. I was surprised how grounded this felt and would love to see more of this from Bravo and Heather.

Suddenly I’m back on team Hall because who is this girl Ashtyn and what is she doing? Also, where was Brandi this entire season? Tired of these 8-episode runs with basically no storyline. An interesting accomplishment when this show is almost overproduced (see: everything about Tyler).
Shockingly, this season was too short with not enough drama. I love Sean, but that was a bizarre arc. Team Brandi forever.
This was…so boring. Maybe because it was just a bunch of white men. Even Betty Gilpin was just…there.

Really consistent and enjoyable, but something about the pacing went left at the end. That’s maybe because I binged it and then had to wait for the finale, but the finale itself felt flatter than I expected from all the lead up.
I wanted more houses, but I will accept the offering as provided. Glad Chrishell is getting out of there before this whole things jumps the shark.
They’ll never make me hate Bre. And on a serious note, this season would’ve been flat without her. I want to like Chelsea so badly, but she’s lowkey anti-women.

Finally, an entry into this universe that actually builds the world and characters into something meaningful. I only wish this could’ve been produced as adult animation, so we could get a bit more meat. Love the animation style and could easily watch this show for many seasons.

I know it’s a procedural, but I want a touch more consistency and continuity if there’s going to be this much at all. The season timeline felt messy, and I’m still unclear if it even matters that people are in pursuit of Charlie. Are we just down for her to be a
migrant, regardless? I do feel like probably.
Feeling a little too Ozark nachos for me. And that Uncut Gems ass finale…Jude and Jason are here, though. I guess I like that.

Another banger of a season. This show is so fresh and reliable in its freshness, which is surprising for a spy dramedy. This crew has really grown on me and I couldn’t probably watch them forever. Long live Roddy Ho.

It feels like they squandered the momentum of the prior season with too much of a time and direction shift. I understand that it’s based off of a book series, but it feels like a missed opportunity for an adaptation to smooth out its events. And I suppose that’s my critique of this whole series already, knowing it’s already renewed for a couple more seasons: it straddles the line too much between series and limited series, without committing to being anthological in any meaningful way. Every interesting self-reference is a whisper, while the others feel like roars. I’m eager for the next season, but not because this one was particularly strong.

They cooked with this season! The story felt much more urgent and was told with greater finesse. The last two episodes, in particular, had me wanting them to slow down (complimentary), so I had more time to savor each setup.
A fine season, but a bit formulaic and didn’t really grab my attention the way I had hoped after the first season. Still love cunty Gary Oldman, though.

I’m not sure if this was actually good, but I like how sleek it was and how in it I felt while watching. I’m curious to see where this goes and I like the brevity of season; everything felt focused and well-paced.
We like it because it’s gritty and well-done, but I’m not going to say it’s discussing or portraying anything revolutionary or in a revolutionary way.
I’m always gonna watch Alexander, but this was so nothing to me. In fairness, I don’t love a heavily narrated show.
We’re in this era where flashy shows use their first season as a prequel and you can feel it, and I hate it here! I love a slow burn, but not quite like this. Give me something to consider for real.
I get it, but I’m not blown away. Often the pacing feels rushed and the result is shallow characters. Curious about Kathryn Hahn in this, surprising exactly no one.

tmdb-tv-14808.name: The Real Housewives of New York City

tmdb.name: The Real Housewives of New York City

reviews.reviewText: Interesting women? Maybe. Interesting show to watch? Not really. A particularly dark end of season mix that suggests filming needs a pause and begins explaining the delay in renewal/premiere announcements.
Something in here is simmering, but the burner needs to get turned up.
That was a doozy to get through. The context of when this season aired makes it even more unreal.
The beginning of the end really popped out this season. And yet, we still live?! Proud of Tinsley for getting out.
Is this the LuAnn redemption arc I was promised? Because all I’m seeing is someone very in the asshole stage of recovery where they’re not actually committed to wellness.
The deterioration of Bethenny’s and Carole’s relationship was hard to watch. And the price was my love of B because huh? Tinsley is growing on me.

Another banger season. Tinsley is annoying and I don’t understand the chokehold Ramona and Lu have over these women, but thank G for B and Dorinda. How is Carole the only one activated in the pre-T era? I’m not shocked, but ouch!

Nothing is really going on, but still a banger of a season. My girl Bethenny is still the only one with any common sense and I think that continues to paint a class story on this show. And in turn, she becomes the closest thing to a reliable narrator. And my queen Carole gets that.

Bethenny’s return feels like a renewed lease. I like how she was able to come in, cut through the noise, and immediately clock the dynamics and engage with them on her own terms. She really is a class in relationship management and is often playing chess when everyone else thinks she’s playing checkers with them. At what point do we get help for Ramona, Sonja, and LuAnn? Because those girls are not getting it. Now I’m a Heather fan, too…an interesting time to be alive. Glad Carole is living her best cougar life.

Okay, so turns out I’m Carole hive and don’t mind Heather as much as I thought. Poor Sonja has lost me and Luann’s absence was a dark omen from the beginning. I don’t care about Kristen, but I hate her husband. It was great to see some iconic moments like the last leg in context. WHO ARE YOU TO GET ME WET?

A sleepy but solidifying season. The cast feels more gelled than during the last entry and I finally have proper housewives of this franchise to dislike in Heather and Aviva. LuAnn has really fallen from grace and Jacques deserves better. But what do I know? Like Sonja, I don’t stir the pot, I stir the drink!

This one had a weird fall off. I started disliking Jill and LuAnn and loved the earnest friendship between Ramona and Sonja. Thankfully, there was less Kelly and Alex. Cindy is no replacement for Bethenny, but I see what they tried to do there.

Kelly should be on a watch list. Amazed that Bethenny is still the most relatable one at this stage. This season was hers and she ate it all up while being much more pulled back. I can see that really bothers the girls! I’m excited to see how Sonja shakes things up more in future seasons.
Everyone woke up at the mid-season turn. I’m curious where they take it from here because Kelly gives Monica Garcia vibes lowkey. I didn’t realize this level of delusion was so enjoyable to watch.
Nothing special, but also iconic at the same time. I can’t explain it. I’m already trying not to become a fan of people I know will give me the ick as I keep going through the series.
This show isn’t gay and neither am I.

Oh, how I’ll miss this show and its chaos. Appreciate that these was a bunch of shitty men and then Seema finding the one good one, who is also the hottest. Very me coded of her. Still waiting for the Goldenblatts spinoff, though.
Lena Dunham knows how to make a show. A little harried in places, but super here for the vibes. Megan really nails it.
I don’t know that there’s much going on here, yet I am still invested. Club Club forever.
Cute, but could’ve been edited into a movie and been more successful. Milly Alcock needs to be booked more.
A marked improvement over the prior season, but still feeling very flat. I think Carmy has worn out his welcome as an unchanging character. I’m wondering if we’re nearing the end of this show’s run.

It feels like this season was weirdly too focused on the craft of making good television episodes that it forgot how to make a good television season. In the end, it amounted to a collection of variably interesting vignettes with an inch of plot progression. For everything that happens, nothing happens. And it makes me wonder if we’ll get a fourth season sooner than expected — the work verges on intentionally flat enough to be setting that sort of surprise up.

Came here for Jack Innanen and wasn’t disappointed. This is reminiscent of Broad City, meaning there are many unremarkable elements because I am not the audience. Like why would he flash his interview panel…
A cute start that kinda fizzled out. I wish they had pushed the absurdity more. Holmes forever!
No one is well, particularly Shannon and Joel Kim Booster. And while it’s not so obvious on the show, what is seen explains their mini feud during filming. Ralph can call me any time.

Honestly pretty good when you don’t have someone yapping in your ear about Joel. Pacing and storyline connectivity could use some work and I’m not sure anyone in here is actually ready to lead a series. But we love Young Mazino on our screens.
We should have a renewal announcement by now, but alas. Are shows allowed to have fun anymore? This was the perfect Ryan Murphy Season 1. In that sense, reminiscent of Scream Queens.

I wish Stassi’s contribution had been more meaningful, by which I mean more chaotic. I appreciate her growth, but we needed more drama. I’m always curious about the undercurrent of homophobia that runs underneath LVP’s many narratives. Are we never going to address that?

I’ll have another one, thank you! I missed LVP orchestrating chaos in my screen and I’m happy to have finally tuned into this. The cast is hot and messy, and yet I don’t care about them individually much. The show itself blends several subgenres in a way that manages to work. And, surprising no one, I love Chef! No notes.

tmdb-tv-285643.name: Denise Richards and Her Wild Things

tmdb.name: Denise Richards and Her Wild Things

reviews.reviewText: I only care about her hot husband and religious daughter. And I suppose that’s enough.
Nothing special, but I love Uzo. Edwina Findley’s Sheila was the surprise delight I didn’t know I needed. The birding cold open with Cordelia’s nephew was the best part of the season.
I technically watched this, but honestly what was there to watch? Hilary Swank was the only light in the darkness of how bad this season was.
It feels like it took this season to wake everyone up to the idea that this show is just “okay” and that White may not be what we thought. Even Carrie Coon couldn’t save it — not that it’s her job.

There is something so satisfying about this show and I think it’s honestly because it feels like people I know. A large part of that is about the stage of life these people are in. And honestly I prefer to see Garcelle here over RHOBH. Also, everyone is hot?

I don’t think the average person offering commentary on this really has the range for the discussion. Because it’s not just about the politics of whose story gets told under the patriarchy, but also the advent of social media technology and its impact on how we communicate and shape community. Stories like this one are a function of how ingrained patriarchy is in the development of these platforms and, at this point, that should be more the point of discussion. We need to root out this bias at the source and, frankly, that source is not really children. There have been bullies and incels and everything between, within, and without those groups for a long time. It is the proliferation of communication and community-building patterns that lead to violence, particularly misogyny-driven violence, that is at issue. Nevertheless, this show was well-acted and the one-take approach for each episode felt like a marvel, rather than a gimmick. If anything, my wish may have been for a couple more episodes exploring some points before and after the story that was presented, to provide deeper context that feels necessary to address this topic.

This is everything you could want out of a season 2 of a show like this. It felt like the resources it deployed were necessary and used to their fullest extent. The expansion of both the breadth and depth of the world, while keeping pace and still leaving room for questions, was a marvel. The cinematography was levelled up in a big way, and the assembly of the work, as a whole, is a testament to the devil being in the details. Dichen Lachman demonstrates, once again, her incredible range, and I hope this leads her and Adam Scott to get their flowers (read: Emmys). Mark S is public enemy #1.

This show is finally cooking with gas! The first season felt like such a slog, compared to this one. I appreciate how they simplified the lexicon of the world a bit and made the events more legible on a first watch than they were in season one. Lee Pace is still very attractive, but it’s really Dini Fetscher that needs to give me a call — whew!

It feels like they bit off more than they can chew at the script level. The plot might work, but the episode by episode focus is lacking and makes it hard to get invested, especially mid-season. What starts strong and what should’ve ended strong is soured by the mediocre middle. Looks beautiful, though (and I’m talking about Lee Pace).

I’m not kidding when I say this is cinema. It’s hard to imagine the series is ending here. The forced beat packaging is suggestive of a peak season with an imminent return. Surely, everyone does not get a happy ending. And yet, I find myself happy for all of them. What to do?

I may have been the only one watching this. To be honest, the Lisa Barlow of it all isn’t enough to make this interesting. I can’t stand when real estate reality television doesn’t bother to balance the drama with actual listings. I find it even more cringe when the drama is about some whack white dude.

This dragged on for 6 episodes to the point that the final one felt meaningless. At least we always know it’s men having the audacity that is the true crime. In the end, that’s not enough to justify a plot where the central issue is made to be miscommunication. I will say that I was particularly impressed by Cate Blanchett’s physical performance. Her collapse in the hospital took the wind out of my sails.
There was a lot of energy to this, but I really didn’t care at any point.

tmdb-tv-134373.name: The Sex Lives of College Girls

tmdb.name: The Sex Lives of College Girls

reviews.reviewText: This show is never really bad, but it still hasn’t figured out consistency. So many of the bits are hilarious and it has plenty of laugh out loud moments, but the storylines feel trivial and lack cohesion. And I guess that what college at a private institution in New England feels like, but should it appear that way in a television show? I  don’t know how they would keep this show going longer than one or two more seasons.

I’m always eager to see a somewhat formulaic show expand its world after an initial season. That said, I think some elements of the original formula need to exist in subsequent seasons, in order to maintain the throughline of a show’s narrative. I’m not certain that this season achieved a proper blend of new and old and suffered for it. The entirety of the last episode is so melodramatic that I thought it was a dream sequence for longer than I care to admit. I’m glad they’ll be wrapping this up with a third season that can hopefully make sense of the mess of this one.

I think this is a solid start, but it doesn’t really build off of the recently established Dune-iverse the way it should. If anything, what’s been established should help shortcut this right into action, but they zoom through their own setup and do not stick the landing. I would’ve preferred an entire season of the past like in “House of the Dragon,” over what this was.
This season finally felt cohesive and consistent. Unfortunately, too little too late. Baby Spencer forever.
I feel like I saw no progression from season one. It may as well have been the same season, in fact.
This show feels like a string of silence, punctuated by the outlier funny line reading.

tmdb-tv-83631.name: What We Do in the Shadows

tmdb.name: What We Do in the Shadows

reviews.reviewText: I’m so sad to see this gang go. This season felt like a particularly strong return to form and a great way to go out, right until the final frame and the credit roll. I laughed, I cried, I questioned my sanity.
I don’t know that this show has bad seasons but this is a recent standout that feels like it’s getting back to the strength of its early days. I’m gonna miss this gang! Justice for Guillermo.

We lost the plot a bit on this one. I feel like the time jump did not serve them, although I know there is the reality of Kaley Cuoco’s actual pregnancy from season one. I hope they get at least one more to bounce back.

Chris Messina is getting hotter. Kaley Cuoco is getting funnier. What’s not to love between that, the tennis aspect of this show, and a murder plot? I’m not really a true crime or podcast person, but now I’m on the edge of locking in.

I think this would be considered prestige television if it had a different title and aired on a different network/service. Stephanie Hsu is a godsend and ground this show; such a performance feels like it should be unobtainable, given the plot, but she proves expert at marrying the balance between beats. I love a good white board moment, too. And the John Early of it all isn’t lost on me.
This was exactly what I wanted it to be: stupid and pointless. I just wish it had been slightly more serious or a fuller callback, rather than whatever this weird operation angle was.

tmdb-tv-274820.name: Cold Case: Who Killed JonBenét Ramsey

tmdb.name: Cold Case: Who Killed JonBenét Ramsey

reviews.reviewText: I think at this point, we need to start following the money of this case, more than anything else. It feels ridiculous that this is such a mystery, but we still think it can be solved, and yet it remains unsolved.

There was so much space for this to be interesting, and then it wasn’t at all. I think more focus on the political aspects would’ve solved that for me, but it seems the show was more focused on shallow relationships.
This went down like water. I know Linda Cardellini has always been a smokeshow, but has Ray Romano always been daddy?
A very bittersweet ending, which feels just right for this tender show. I’m going to miss these characters, their rawness, and their softness, for a while. I may have cried at “The Climb.”
So wholesome. Scared for the relationships in Season 3.
Part of me feels like this show is getting better to me, but then I reflect on it and it never really progresses past witty zingers.
They saved all the jokes for the season finale! At least it’s picking up?
It took me months to slog through this opening season. I can’t tell if it’s the show itself or just the fact that it’s so of its time. It’s not bad…it’s just not good?
In the end, this feels like every Tina Fey-produced show that can’t keep up with its original concept without quickly growing stale. A large part of that has to do with her husband’s scoring.
Renée Elise Goldsberry might be in peak form, but I can already feel the show stagnating.
They tore! I wasn’t ready for just how good this would be. I did find myself wanting more Ashley Park, though.
It’s too bad that this took so long to get good. By the end, I wanted more, but the first several episodes have a generic malaise that doesn’t go beyond the content itself.

I feel like this show never gets over itself. In trying to be aware of how shitty of a person its creator is, it never actually deals with it. That said, it’s well-produced and maybe what I like the least is all the pain I see in it and find resonant within myself.
This show was never afraid to get weird. And I’m grateful for that.
This show never really found its groove. It had consistently funny bits, but it never felt consistently funny in its entirety.
Too cheesy, even for me. I’m glad Ted is still getting gigs, though!
Yea…no.
This is definitely a saccharine Netflix show, but I ate every minute up. Justine Lupe hive, rise up!

This starts off with a strong premise, then never really goes anywhere. Good for her, but I’m seeing the illusion of growth rather than actual growth and character development. An atypical request from me: there could have been more fantastical elements or scenes.
This felt like a precipitous drop off from the first season. The aspect ratio shifts felt less balanced and the show became less interesting. I’m not even sure that I cared, in the end.

I have to get it out of the way — I’m from Worcester. Whatever place these people are in, it’s not that. The aesthetics are close, but not quite right, as are the accents; the place where this is most obvious is actually the casting because it misses the true socioeconomic underpinnings of the city by having the series fronted by white actors. That said, I think there’s a hell of a lot to love. Annie Murphy is crushing it and I’m surprisingly enthralled by Mary Hollis Inboden. The cinematography theatrics never get tired, which also shocked me. I’m looking forward to the back half of the series.

I can’t say this was a good show, but I’m glad it was an Aidan Gallagher and Ritu Arya vehicle. As the driving acting force in the series, I’m really glad they got their character arc just to eat a little bit, even if it made no sense. Megan Mullally and Nick Offerman were great as always, but I’m still convinced the writers have had beef with Justin Min for a minute. Thankfully, they gave us Tom Hopper Magic Mike.

A little contrived, but Jake Gyllenaal has never been hotter. It was nice to see Lily Rabe and Peter Sarsgaard put in work, but this was the Bill Camp and Ruth Negga show and that’s that. The last 15 minutes ate down in a cute way.

Julio is a little freak and I love it. If there’s one thing this show does well, it’s build on the ethereal and esoteric world of Julio. I imagine it would be hard to make sense of this without having seen his prior work. That’s also the delight in it, though. It feels like an inside joke, written as a love letter to those that dare to pay attention to the world around them. Heavy plus for the expert use of Bowen Yang, Tomás Matos, and Dylan O’Brien.

I enjoyed most of this season, but I still need them to pick up the pace. The stakes seem appropriately set, but then the characters are not set up to action on them in any meaningful way. Or at least the audience does not see enough of those actions. The scene with Rhaenyra and Alicent in the sept, revealing how much is misunderstood between them, sticks out. As does the fourth episode. Melys and Rhaenys forever.

Surprisingly grounded. I liked this much more than I thought I would and I’ll be thinking about those girls and Thunderstruck for a long time. That said, Kelli Finglass might be evil. I’m very wary of her.

Watching this with the context of RHOBH is something. It feels like somewhere in the midst of everything, Mauricio just became an asshole? I know people were clamoring about seeing “more” on this show, regarding his separation from Kyle, but I’m not certain that is to his benefit. He seems like a man child going through a midlife crisis. He’s still hot, though!

RHOBH plus Selling Sunset? I’m in! Surprisingly, Farrah is the only one  with any sense. She’s the least known family member as I watch this, but definitely the savviest. That said, this “agency” is incredibly messy. As a result, this show is giving Succession for Dummies…which I’m not mad at.

This was an enjoyable watch, though easily a more passive one. For all the special interest, something about it was delivered in a way that felt exclusive, bordering on inaccessible unless you’re a certain type of Black person.

Goodbye to this okay show. It’s so unfortunate that this season continued to prove that Barrett Doss should have been the lead. Her chemistry with Jay Hayden is unparalleled and their characters are the undeniable heart of the show. I am rooting for them to get a spinoff or maybe even step into new roles on another network. They deserve (even if Hayden isn’t actually queer…).

I was triggered nearly the entirety of this, but I also couldn’t look away. I think it captured the emotional complexity of living within an abusive system as an abuse survivor. Nava Mau should be in everything.
I had to take my time and savor this one, but I blew through the last four. They need season 2 immediately because they pissed me off with the ending. Toranaga is that bitch!!

I wish this whole thing had been as interesting as the last 20 minutes, especially given that it’s a limited series. It never really found its tone nor what it had to say. A rare miss from Ms. Winslet.
This felt like an uncharacteristically weak season in that it felt without its typical human angle. The last two episodes were a nice recovery.

D&D are back to prove they’re hungry after the much maligned last season of Game of Thrones. In the end, they’re only half successful. They know how to showrun and that’s clear, but the overall pacing of the show feels choppy. It’s unfortunate that this is the case for a work dealing so closely with temporal concepts. The acting is pretty alright, but the more compelling stories and performances are sadly relegated to backstory and B plot. I’m hoping they have enough slack either way Netflix to get to the point where the show is fully working and not just setting itself up. I’m reminded that it took me 3 seasons to finally get into Game of Thrones.

tmdb-tv-245902.name: Quiet on Set: The Dark Side of Kids TV

tmdb.name: Quiet on Set: The Dark Side of Kids TV

reviews.reviewText: This definitely kept my attention, but mostly because I was lost in the framing. While it doesn’t excuse the behavioral patterns identified in the research behind the series, it does feel like a PR campaign for Drake Bell. It’s also difficult to look away from the fact that so many Black actors are speaking up about witnessed behaviors that are less extreme than the ones the series is trying to draw the most attention to. It’s difficult to believe that a body of, theoretically, more vulnerable children didn’t experience those horrors. In that, it feels like the framing is eliding over certain truths that it shouldn’t. But maybe that’s just my personal speculation.

Mid is not accurate enough, but I’m also too ambivalent to be more upset. For all the hype, this really was just a flop. There was no gravitas in the story to ever make me care about what was going on.
Susan Sarandon kinda ate Jessica Lange up. And I’m always here for Ms. Shipka.
Sofia is great, and the story is well-executed, but the script and acting aren’t always present. Nevermind the weak translations.
Episode 5 is a standout that makes me wish the entire show had been focused that way. That said, Sarah Blue will always eat. Nicole Kidman felt surprisingly (read: appropriately) muted.

They chewed with this one. We love a revenge plot and we love Fiona Shaw. Jodi and Kali both chewed; I could’ve watched their characters together for many more cases. The biggest drawback was honestly some of the choices for the music.
The bookend episodes were amazing. Realistically, though, this could’ve been a smaller limited series or a movie.
It took a while to find its groove, but the back half is a treat! Maya Erskine forever! (And let’s not forget Parker Posey.)

Not in love with the animation style, but the world they created is stunning if not frightening. A great story that I immediately want more of. Voice acting reminded me a lot of The Midnight Gospel (in a good way).

Slipped and fell into a rewatch on this one. It still manages to hold up, which I did not expect. It’s not really a good script, but the story has staying power. And honestly the tale of a power hungry genius playing god isn’t one we get bored of.
This was so cute. Loved the animation style and honestly could’ve eaten up at least 20 more episodes!

For a two episode season, it lacks a punch. It’s sad that this was where this version stopped because you can see all of the potential and seeds for the future versions. I could’ve watched an extended version of this season for hours.
Interesting to see where this was and was not translated to form the original US port over. The grooming relationship of it all is always so…whew.
Can a melodrama be bad? Not clear, but this season sure did aim for that to be the case.

tmdb-tv-134095.name: A Murder at the End of the World

tmdb.name: A Murder at the End of the World

reviews.reviewText: The show started with so much promise. By the end, I was not as gripped. The final reveal, in particular, felt contrived. Maybe it’s because this is my area of expertise, where law meets tech and media. I would’ve much rather have spent the back half of this show in the court room if this was its angle all along.

It took me 3 attempts just to finish this show. It gets lost in trying to paint intricate details into a straightforward story. As a result, its narrative gets lost and none of the acclaimed talent walk away having given their typical quality performances.
